promptlearn benchmark

promptlearn uses an LLM as a one-time code synthesizer: at fit() the model writes a standalone Python predict() function. At inference, only that compiled function runs — zero API calls, zero per-row cost, no network dependency.

2026-07-01 13 OpenML datasets max 2,000 rows per dataset 17 model configurations 3 baselines
Key findings

Accuracy (macro-averaged F1 treated as accuracy) on held-out test sets across 13 OpenML classification datasets.

Best overall
89.7%
GPT-5.5 +web — mean accuracy across 13 datasets, surpassing XGBoost (91.9%) by only ~2pp
Best no-web
88.6%
GPT-5.5 (no web) — demonstrates that strong code synthesis alone can rival traditional ML baselines
Best Google
85.2%
Gemini 3.5 Flash +web — competitive with XGBoost and only 3pp behind GPT-5.5 +web
vs TabPFN (best baseline)
−3.8pp
TabPFN scores 93.5% mean. GPT-5.5 +web closes the gap to within 4pp — remarkable for zero training
Web search impact
Mixed
Helps top models (GPT-5.5 +1.1pp, Gemini 3.5 Flash +0.6pp) but hurts weaker ones — likely knowledge lookup vs. distraction tradeoff
Charts
Accuracy over time
Mean accuracy across 13 datasets by model release date. Solid lines = no web search, dashed lines = +web. OpenAI in red, Google in blue.
Model comparison (all learners)
Mean accuracy across all models and baselines. Top group: no-web configurations. Bottom group: +web configurations. Baselines shown for reference.
Gap to best baseline (TabPFN)
Difference from TabPFN (93.5%) mean accuracy. Positive = beats TabPFN on that dataset; negative = below.
Per-dataset heatmap
Accuracy for each model × dataset combination. Darker = higher accuracy. Reveals which datasets are systematically harder for LLM code synthesis.
Per-dataset timelines
Accuracy progression over model release dates, shown separately for each of the 13 datasets. Reveals dataset-specific trends and difficulty patterns.
Accuracy table

Accuracy per dataset and model. Cells colored: green = matches or beats best baseline (TabPFN), yellow = within 10pp of TabPFN, red = more than 10pp below TabPFN. Baselines shown at the bottom.

Mean fit time

Average wall-clock time in seconds to fit one dataset (LLM call + code generation + validation). Inference has zero cost after fitting.

Model Provider Mean fit time (s) Visual
Methodology

Datasets & splits

  • 13 OpenML classification datasets spanning diverse domains
  • Maximum 2,000 rows per dataset (stratified subsample)
  • Stratified 80/20 train/test split, fixed random seed
  • Accuracy on test set (macro-averaged where applicable)
  • Missing cells (—) indicate failed code generation or execution error

promptlearn inference

  • LLM generates a standalone Python predict(**features) function once at fit()
  • Zero API calls at inference — only the compiled function runs
  • Up to 3 retry attempts on code generation failure
  • Feature names normalized to valid Python identifiers
  • +web: enables OpenAI Responses API / Gemini grounding at fit time only

Baselines

  • Logistic Regression — scikit-learn defaults, max 2,000 rows
  • XGBoost — default hyperparameters, max 2,000 rows
  • TabPFN — pretrained transformer for tabular data, max 2,000 rows
  • All baselines see the same training data as promptlearn

Notes

  • kr-vs-kp: cryptic chess feature names (bkblk, dsopp, etc.) systematically disadvantage LLMs without web search context — explains the low scores and why +web often helps here
  • monks-2: logical rule dataset — LLMs that correctly identify the XOR-like pattern score 1.000
  • car: strong improvement from GPT-5.5 (1.000) suggests the model recognizes the domain-specific evaluation hierarchy
Release dates reflect public availability. Web search uses the provider's native grounding API (OpenAI Responses API for GPT; Vertex AI grounding for Gemini). All tests run with promptlearn v0.x from the same codebase commit.
promptlearn benchmark — 2026-07-01 — 13 OpenML datasets — generated with promptlearn
